title: Chapter 56: The Bridge
content: The twelve hours before dawn were the longest of my life.

Colt arrived with eight fighters at the three-hour mark—Gonzales, Harmon, Pratt, and five others whose names I didn't know. They came armed and armored, their Command System coordinating their movements with the eerie precision of a military unit that didn't need to speak. Colt himself looked like he hadn't slept since our meeting, but his eyes were clear and his jaw was set.
"You were right," he said by way of greeting. "The eastern perimeter went dark three hours ago. Our scouts saw them—three hundred, moving in formation. Like an army."
"Two hundred ninety-nine now. Mia killed one."
"One down, two hundred ninety-nine to go." Colt surveyed the Compound's defensive layout—the fortified windows, the layered chokepoints, the rooftop positions. "Not bad. Better than the Roosevelt."
"The architect designed it."

"The irony isn't lost on me." He turned to face me directly. "Here are my terms. My people operate independently. No integration into your chain of command. We hold the eastern approach while your people hold the northeast. If things go sideways, we fall back to the Roosevelt together—I'm not leaving my people to die for your compound."
"Agreed. And if things go well?"
Colt's expression flickered—something that might have been hope, quickly suppressed. "Then we'll talk about what 'well' looks like."
Good enough. I'd take a grudging ally over a righteous enemy any day.
The defensive plan came together in pieces, each one a negotiation between Colt's tactical precision and our group's improvisational flexibility.

Mia and one of Colt's marksmen—a quiet woman named Torres—would share rooftop overwatch, their fields of fire overlapping to cover the three approach vectors the Omegas would likely use. Jessica and Pratt would manage the ground-floor barriers, funneling the horde into kill zones where crossbows and improvised explosives could do maximum damage. Rose would operate from the medical bay, with Sarah as her assistant, ready for casualties.

Rick and Maria would handle logistics—ammunition distribution, water, food, medical supplies. They weren't fighters, but the siege would last hours, and fighters who ran out of arrows or water were just targets with attitude.
Derek volunteered.
He appeared in the common room as the final preparations were underway, his face haggard, his movements careful—the body language of a man who knew he wasn't welcome but couldn't stay away.
"I can fight," he said. Not to me—to Sarah, who was organizing the medical supplies with brisk efficiency.
Sarah didn't look up. "You can hold a door. There's a barricade on the eastern stairwell that needs weight behind it."
"That's not—"

"It's what I'm giving you." Sarah finally met his eyes, and whatever she saw there made her pause. "You want to help? Then help. Don't ask me to trust you with a weapon. Don't ask me to pretend the last six weeks didn't happen. Stand at that door and hold it, and if it breaks, you be the first thing the zombies see. That's your job. That's your *only* job."
Derek swallowed. Nodded. Went to the eastern stairwell without another word.

Sarah watched him go, and I saw the conflict on her face—the part of her that would never forgive, warring with the part that had chosen to become a healer instead of an avenger. She'd chosen mercy. That didn't mean she'd chosen trust.
"He's trying," I said quietly.

"I know." Her voice was threaded with something that wasn't quite anger. "That's the worst part. If he were still a monster, I could hate him cleanly. But he's trying to be better, and that makes it complicated." She looked at me. "I don't do complicated, Alex. I survived Derek by keeping things simple. Now he's making it complicated, and I don't know whether to be grateful or furious."
"Both seems reasonable."

"Both is exhausting." But the corner of her mouth twitched—almost a smile—and Sarah Mitchell went back to organizing bandages with the steady hands of someone who'd decided that survival mattered more than feelings.
Luna found me on the roof at midnight.

The city spread below us in darkness—no streetlights, no neon, no headlights. Just the moon casting silver over ruins and the distant sound of the horde, still hours away, a low murmur like a river that was learning to flood.
"Can you hear them?" I asked.

Luna stood beside me, her amber eyes reflecting moonlight. "Always now. The signal doesn't stop. Even when I push it down, I can feel it—pressure in my skull, like a headache that never quite arrives."
"Is it getting worse?"

"No. Different." She tilted her head, listening to something I couldn't perceive. "The two remaining Omegas are consolidating their forces. The northern one and the waterfront one are moving toward a rendezvous point—halfway between their territories, about six blocks east. They'll merge into a single horde and attack together."
"Can you tell when they'll move?"
"Three AM. They're waiting for the moon to set. Maximum darkness."
Mia would need to know. I keyed the radio and passed the intel—three AM, combined assault from the east. Mia acknowledged with a click.
"Can they hear you?" I asked Luna. "The Omegas. When you're receiving their signal, can they detect you?"
Luna was quiet for a long time. The question hung between us, heavy with implications I didn't want to face.

"I don't know," she finally said. "I've been careful. Listening without responding. But when Mia killed the first Omega—when I felt it die—there was a moment where I... reacted. A spike of emotion, of *satisfaction*, that I couldn't suppress. If they're monitoring the frequency the way I am..."
"They might know you're here."

"They might know what I am." Luna turned to face me, and in the moonlight, her amber eyes held depths I'd never seen—fear and determination and something fierce and protective. "Which means they might be coming here not just for the Compound, but for me. A receiver who can hear their plans. A hybrid who chose the human signal over the Omega signal. I'm a threat to them, Alex. And threats get eliminated first."
"Then we protect you."

"By putting me in the middle of the building, away from windows, while everyone else fights?" Luna shook her head. "No. I'm not going to hide while my family dies defending me. I'm going to fight, and I'm going to listen, and I'm going to give this group every advantage I can."
"Luna—"

"I'm not asking permission." Her hand found mine, her grip fierce. "I'm telling you. I spent weeks as a thing that couldn't choose. Now I can, and I choose to stand with the people I love. That's not negotiable."

I wanted to argue. I wanted to lock her in the safest room in the Compound and stand between her and the horde until my legs gave out. I wanted to protect her the way I'd protected her since Day One—carefully, desperately, with food and warmth and patience.

But Luna wasn't the zombie in the hallway anymore. She was a person making a choice, and if I overrode that choice because I was scared, I'd be no better than the system that tried to manufacture her feelings.
"All right," I said. "But you stay near me. Not because I'm protecting you—because we fight better together."
"Always," Luna said, and the word was a promise older than the apocalypse.
**AFFECTION UPDATE: LUNA — 91%**
The signal pulsed in her head, the horde shifted in the darkness, and dawn crept closer with every heartbeat. Three hours until contact.
Three hours until we found out what the Omegas were really made of.
And whether our alliance—fragile, suspicious, built on necessity rather than trust—could withstand the fire.
